steps.prompt: How big is one corner of a regular pentagon?
steps.subtitle: A polygon is a closed shape made of straight sides, and a regular pentagon has 5 equal corners. On a scale from 0° (flat) to 180° (a straight line), drag the bar to where you think one [interior angle](blue) sits. A square corner is 90°.

feedback.incorrect: A pentagon's corner is obtuse — wider than a square corner (90°) but well short of a straight line (180°).

steps.revealText: Each corner of a regular pentagon is 108°. The five angles add to (5 − 2) × 180° = 540°, and 540° ÷ 5 = 108°. To see why, let's total up a polygon's angles.

steps.prompt: Add sides and watch the angle sum climb
steps.subtitle: Start from the triangle (180°) and tap + to add sides. Each new side gives the shape one more triangle's worth of angle, so the Angle sum jumps by 180° every time.

steps.prompt: However many sides n a polygon has, its interior angles always add up to…
steps.subtitle: You watched the Angle sum jump by 180° for every extra side. Which rule matches that pattern?

options.label: n × 180°
options.feedback: Test it on a square: 4 × 180° = 720°, but four square corners only make 360°. You have to subtract 2 from n first.

options.label: 360°, no matter what
options.feedback: A square totals 360°, but the hexagon above already totals 720° — the sum keeps climbing as you add sides.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Every polygon splits into n − 2 triangles, and each triangle holds 180°.
feedback.incorrect: Each extra side adds one more triangle — another 180°. The rule is (n − 2) × 180°.

steps.prompt: Proof: slice the polygon into triangles

blanks.prompt: From one corner of the hexagon, draw a diagonal to every corner you can reach (not itself or its two neighbours). How many diagonals is that?
blanks.hint: 6 corners − the corner itself − its 2 neighbours = 3.

blanks.prompt: Those diagonals fan the hexagon into how many [triangles](green)?
blanks.hint: An n-gon splits into n − 2 triangles; here n = 6, so 6 − 2.

blanks.prompt: Each of the 4 [triangles](green) holds 180°, so the hexagon's angles total 4 × 180° = ?

stages: Take any polygon — here a hexagon — and pick a single corner.
Draw a diagonal from that corner to every other corner it can reach. A hexagon needs three of them.
The diagonals slice the hexagon into 4 [triangles](green), and every interior angle of the hexagon is built from those triangle angles — none is left over.
Each triangle holds 180°, so the total is 4 × 180° = 720°. In general an n-gon splits into n − 2 [triangles](green): (n − 2) × 180°.

steps.prompt: Work out one corner of a regular decagon
steps.intro: A regular decagon has [10 equal sides](blue), so n = 10. Find one [interior angle](blue) the way the formula does it — one move at a time.
steps.subtitle: First total all the angles with (n − 2) × 180°, then split that total evenly across the 10 corners.

parts.prompt: How many triangles does a decagon split into? That's n − 2 = 10 − 2 = ?
parts.note: 10 − 2 = 8 triangles fan out from one corner.

parts.prompt: Each triangle holds 180°, so the angles total 8 × 180° = ?
parts.note: The interior angles of a regular decagon add to 1440°.

parts.prompt: All 10 corners are equal, so divide: 1440° ÷ 10 = ?
parts.hint: Split the total evenly across 10 corners.
parts.note: Each interior angle of a regular decagon is 144°.

steps.doneNote: That's the whole method: (n − 2) × 180° for the sum, then ÷ n for one corner of a regular polygon.

problems.prompt: Find the sum of the interior angles of a pentagon (5 sides).

feedback.incorrect: The interior angles of an n-gon add to (n − 2) × 180°.

problems.prompt: Find each interior angle of a regular hexagon (6 sides).

feedback.incorrect: Each interior angle of a regular n-gon is (n − 2) × 180° ÷ n.

problems.prompt: Find each exterior angle of a regular octagon (8 sides).

feedback.correct: Correct. The exterior angles always total 360°, so each is 360° ÷ 8 = 45°.
feedback.incorrect: The exterior angles of any polygon add to 360°, so each is 360° ÷ n.

steps.subtitle: The interior angles of any n-gon add to ([n](blue) − 2) × 180°. Fill in the pentagon case (n = 5).

feedback.incorrect: A pentagon has n = 5 sides. Each of the n − 2 = 3 triangles holds 180°, so multiply 3 × 180°.

steps.doneNote: (5 − 2) × 180° = 540° — and dividing by 5 equal corners gives the 108° you estimated at the start.
